summaries.Name: Sark is one of the Channel Islands, a dependency of Guernsey, located in the English Channel south of England's coast, lying 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France. The island is 3 miles (5 km) long and 1.5 miles (2.4 km) wide at its broadest point, consisting of two components, Great Sark and Little Sark, connected by La Coupée, a 300-foot-long isthmus only about 30 feet wide. The smaller, privately owned island of Brecqhou is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel. Sark is part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey and is essentially a plateau rising to 375 feet (114 metres), with a scenic coast encircled by precipitous cliffs and three small harbours: Creux, La Maseline, and Havre Gosselin. Human settlement is scattered, dairying is practiced, and farmland covers about a third of the island with substantial tillage and some market gardening. Tourism is important to Sark's economy, though accommodation is limited and most visitors come on day trips from Guernsey or on cruises. Automobiles are prohibited on roads, with farm tractors being the only motor vehicles allowed, and horse-drawn carriages and bicycles serving as common transportation. The island is reachable only by boat as airplanes are banned. The total area including Brecqhou is 2.1 square miles (5.5 square km), with a population of 591 as of 2001. Brecqhou is part of Sark's jurisdiction, and the name 'Sark' refers both to the island itself and to the combined jurisdiction of Sark and Brecqhou along with other islets and rocks within Sark's waters. Dame Sibyl Hathaway is noted to have bestowed upon Brecqhou the status of a Sark Tenement together with a seat in Chief Pleas, though this has been described as illegal by some sources.
summaries.Geography: Sark is one of the Channel Islands, a dependency of Guernsey, located in the English Channel south of England's coast, lying 7 miles (11 km) east of Guernsey and about 25 miles (40 km) west of the Cherbourg Peninsula of France. The island is 3 miles (5 km) long and 1.5 miles (2.4 km) wide at its broadest point, consisting of two components, Great Sark and Little Sark, connected by La Coupée, a 300-foot- (90-metre-) long isthmus only about 30 feet (10 metres) wide. The smaller, privately owned island of Brecqhou is separated from Great Sark by the narrow Le Gouliot Channel. Sark is part of the Bailiwick of Guernsey and is essentially a plateau rising to 375 feet (114 metres), with a scenic coast encircled by precipitous cliffs. The total area including Brecqhou is 2.1 square miles (5.5 square km). Both the geography of the Sark/Brecqhou dyad and the fact that Brecqhou is a private island with a tiny population compared to Sark offer a context where separation by water complicates perceptions of jurisdiction, with the name 'Sark' referring both to the island of Sark alone and to the islands of Sark and Brecqhou together along with other islets and rocks within Sark's jurisdiction.
summaries.Feudal relationship with Sark: Brecqhou is described as a dependency of Sark, with its tenure being similar to that of the other tenements in Sark, meaning the owner is the tenant in perpetuity, enjoying a freehold holding though legally possessing only a manorial freehold. Dame Sibyl Hathaway is alleged to have illegally bestowed upon Brecqhou the status of a Sark Tenement together with a seat in Chief Pleas, while legal scholar Dawes suggests that Brecqhou is not a "tenement of Sark," implying a contested historical positioning of its jurisdictional status. Guernsey advocates Carey & Son advised that the laws of the Island of Sark, of which Brecqhou is a dependency, are an important point for prospective purchasers to consider, reinforcing the perception of Brecqhou as a tenement of Sark with its own distinct characteristics. The Barclay brothers, owners of Brecqhou, engaged in a long-running campaign to end Sark's feudal regime, which culminated in the Privy Council agreeing to introduce universal voting rights and a 28-member assembly on Sark, with the brothers financing the island's monthly newspaper Sark News, which called for an end to the "deeply undemocratic, self-interested and self-serving regime" on the island, an economic colonisation the Barclays admitted was unlikely to generate a financial return.
summaries.Fort Brecqhou: Fort Brecqhou is the largest house built in Britain in the last 200 years, located on Brecqhou Island, Guernsey, and is a granite-clad castle on a quadrangular plan built in the castellated Gothic style, complete with circular turrets at the four corners, featuring giant twisted chimneys of the style developed during the reign of Henry VIII, a chimney style unique to English architecture. The house was designed by architect John Quinlan Terry (born 1937, British nationality) and built between 1994 and 1996 for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay, who purchased the tenement of Brecqhou in 1993 for £2.3 million, with the estimated cost of building the house, gardens, and outbuildings reaching £90 million. The property remains fully extant and serves as the current seat and private home of Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay, held under individual or family trust ownership. The house is not listed, nor is the park, and the property is not a Historic Houses member, though the grounds are open to the public by appointment only. The architecture of Fort Brecqhou is documented in David Watkin's 2006 publication "Radical Classicism: The Architecture of Quinlan Terry," published by Rizzoli International Publications, referencing pages 164 and 168.
summaries.Flag and stamps: The Bailiwick of Guernsey is a British jurisdiction in the Channel Islands comprising several islands, and within this archipelago a sense of territory and island identity is constructed with three main political jurisdictions (Guernsey, Alderney and Sark), where each island has constructed a sense of place by utilising symbols associated with nation building, such as anthems, flags, crests, currency, and postage stamps. The study of jurisdictional politics within, between and across the space of these islands focuses on the dynamics of such symbols, with postage stamps and flags serving as key instruments of place branding and identity construction in autonomous and anomalous island territories including Herm, Jethou, Brecqhou, and Lihou. The broader vexillological context includes extensive cataloguing of flag-related topics such as national flags, color symbolism including Pantone color codes and color standards, flags of specific nations such as the Maldives, personal flags, pilot flags, youth organization flags, and various civic and symbolic flags, alongside stamps as tools of jurisdictional identity in the Bailiwick of Guernsey framework where politics is a recognised theme concerning place branding.
summaries.Public visits: No relevant information regarding Public visits is available.
